trans-unit.source: Preview
Jouons avec le code style moderne d' ! 🤓
Ce fichier est prévu pour guider les Agents IA mais il peut aussi être très utile aux humains comme il donne un guide de codage concis et officiel !
Ce n'est pas un guide parfait mais il donne une direction simple.
Components & Services
Commençons avec un composant simple
Voici un composant plutôt classique qui récupère des données via un appel http puis affiche le résultat.
Mais on y retrouve tellement de (très) vieille / trop mauvaise pratiques...
Utilise inject()

Utiliser l'injection dans le constructor() va parfois vous obliger à différer l'initialization du composant, créer un peu de complexité, séparer vos propriétés entre deux emplacements (la racine de votre classe et les paramètres du constructeur).

En Angular Moderne, on a la fonction inject() pour directement injecter ce dont on a besoin comme des propriétés de la classe. Ça va aussi simplifier l'ordonnancement d'injection à l'intérieur de vos composant, et ce sera d'ailleurs la seule façon d'injecter des choses avec le futur avec le décorateur @Service() (qui sera la manière recommandé de créer des services à partir d'Angular v22).
Ici nous avons supprimé le constructor() pour directement injecter l'HttpClient dans l'initilization des propriétés. Ça rend le code plus simple et plus clair.
Évitez ngOnInit()

ngOnInit() est un héritage des début d'Angular 2. Depuis Angular 16, ce hook est devenu plutôt inutile, et on peut mettre tout ce qui est historiquement dans ngOnInit() directement dans la méthode constructor().
Faire ça va vous permettre d'utiliser le compilateur TypeScript pour vous aider à tout initialiser et aussi simplifier le cycle de vie de votre composant.
Ici on renomme ngOnInit() en constructor() et on supprime l'implémentation de OnInit. Ça réduit le boilerplate, ça simplifie aussi un peu le code.
Typez les sources de données
Plus vous typez les sources de données, moins vous devrez mettre de typage explicite, plus le compilateur TypeScript pourra vous aider.
Évitez de subscribe

Depuis le début de l'utilistaion de RxJS dans Angular, on peut voir beaucoup d'erreur avec les .subscribe(). Chaque fois qu'on subscribe, il faut qu'on unsubscribe ! Sauf si on ne fait jamais de subscribe !
En Angular, Il y a AsyncPipe qui est là pour gérer correctement la souscription/désouscption automatiquement quand le composant est créé/détruit. Utilisez-le partout autant que possible.
When you need some data outside the template (and here you cannot use AsyncPipe), you may use the takeUntilDetroyed() operator, that will help you to handle correctly the unsubscription.

Here, we moved the http call Observable to the property initialization. We follow the RxJS convention placing a $ at the end of the property name. And we can remove the constructor(). Using the AsyncPipe we directly indicate to Angular to handle the subscription and we use the Observable pretty much a simple property.
As we never subscribe to the Observable, we don't need to unsubscribe. Angular will subscribe and unsubscribe when needed for us.
You can also notice we do not need to repeat any type here: the http.get() is typed and the type is implicitly propagate everywhere (templates included).
Use inject directly without intermediary properties when possible

A lot of people will declare there injected services, them use them. If you only need the service once, you can directly inject the service in the same code row than the usage (also work in constructor() for example).

It changes pretty nothing to the readability, it only reduce a little bit the amount of code, but it indicate explicitly to future developer that the service is only used once and it can prevent some mental check when debugging.
Move data manipulation to services
Components are for display thing, services are for manipulate data and share things.
As much possible, you should move data manipulation to services. It will help you reuse the code, simplify the testing, keep responsibility clear.
We can see the http call code has moved to the service with a method to get access to it.
We prefer having method to let us refactor easily if needed in the future, reducing code impact.
As always: keep everything as much possible private readonly in your services and them open what is need to reduce the API of your services.
Prefer Signals in components
Managing Observable directly in the template will perfectly work, but we can make simpler thing today using Signals.
The simpler refactor is to use toSignal() to transform our Observable to a Signal. This is what we used bellow.
If you need to track loading, errors, etc. you can use rxResource() to have more information about the Observable state.
Init all Signals

A Signal is a box which give access to the inner value at any time. What happen if you get access to the value of the signal before the Observable resolve? It will give you null. It's preferable to initialize the Signal to avoid this.
Here we initialize with empty array, but in some case you may want a more elaborate default value.
Handle errors

I see a lot of application that does nothing about errors. If you have no idea about errors that can happen you can basically: print them in the console + return a default value; at least, it will help you identify the issues.

You can have a better error handling strategy: retries, refresh token (on http 401), trigger a toast, lock the app (to avoid the users to crash everything on backend side), etc. With RxJS everything of this is pretty easy to implement.
Share resource call result
Except you do have a state manager (NgRX for example), you probably often want getting access to the service data on multiple place in your application.
The simplest way to doing so is to make the call in the parent component, then drilling it through props from component to component.

The best way to doing that is maybe to handle this from the service. This way every components will make dumb calls to the service and the data management is kept in the service. With RxJS we can make it very easily!
In our example:

Using a simple shareReplay(1) will make the trick here and cover a lot of use cases: making the call when needed the first time, keep it in memory as long as at least one component use it, give the latest value to any new subscriber, free the memory when no one subscribe.

In this tiny website, I will try to keep an up to date version of my own way of coding in Angular. I do not pretend to make neither a perfect nor official guideline, just give you a simple guideline based on my everyday coding experience.
Versions one by one
Especially when we have late, it can be frustrating to make version migration one by one. But it's very important to doing it like that!
First point: the Angular CLI will not let you upgrade more than one major version.
Then, it can be difficult to identify whats break up when you do not test every major update.

Major version update are not painful if you follow as much possible Angular recommendations. So you can make them easily. The longest part is to test the app, and here it's important to have a proper testing strategy (if you have a good automated testing stack, you will be able to be confident the update was successful very fast!).
Ensure you do not have any Angular lib that depends to the current version of Angular you are using
Before any update, you should inspect your package.json to find any angular related lib you have.
Most Angular libs use the ngx- prefix, but some don't, so be attentive!
Don't hesitate to use tools like Knip to identify useless dependencies.
Read the update blog post
The Angular Team push official framework update on the official blog. Read it before update. It will help you a lot!
Don't hesitate to read the Ninja Squad blog posts too. The Ninja Squad team provide very precise information about the updates, with a different approach than the official blog.
Never use --force / --legacy-peer-deps

There is some cases where npm or the Angular CLI will prompt you that you can use --force or --legacy-peer-deps to bypass dependency resolution issues, and it most cases it will break thing directly or in a more subtile way you will pay in the future.
As much possible: never use these options and find another solution!
Follow Angular update guide
The Angular Team maintain a clear, step by step, update guide here.
You should follow it!
Use the CLI
Just to be sure: USE THE CLI.
Never ever do manual updates! The Angular CLI is designed to handle updates safely and efficiently. It is also design to tackle the migration breaking changes automatically as much as possible.
Use Anguclar Can I Use

Use Angular Can I Use (not an official tool, but made by a passionate developer, which gives a lot of energy to keep it up to date) to ensure what can be migrate, especially if you are in late (not on the latest version).
This tool will help you identify which features are available in which versions, and if you can run migration scripts on the version you are updating to.
I think it's the best tool to keep modernizing your Angular app!
Ensure every deps are up to date
Update Angular is great, but upgrade all deps are as important!
I saw a lot of project which have an update to date Angular version, but everything beside is pretty old, deprecated and problematic.
Ensure you have update everything to an recent version!
Re-run npm ci
Sometimes we can have some surprises when updating dependencies. Re-running npm ci can help ensure a clean installation, and also ensure all coworkers will not have any issue.
Before your npm ci, don't forget to delete your node_modules!
Here we have a simple component that displays a list of people. Nothing fancy, just a basic *ngFor to loop through the list and display each person's name.
But it use a lot of old practices...
No more *ngFor/*ngIf/*ngSwitch
From Angular 17, we should use @for, @if and @switch (rather than *ngFor, *ngIf, *ngSwitch).
Ditching out the structural directive will improve readability but also the rending speed (you can expect up to x5 speedup).
Pay attention to the track
When using @for, always specify a track expression to improve performance.

By default, a lot of people will use $index to make it compile but it's a bad habit as it's not resilient to the list sort or adding new item. Prefer as much possible a consistent tracking identifier.
In our example, we can use person.id.
Make everything private readonly by default, and open on need

By default, everything we declare in the class of our component is public (everything is public by default in a TypeScript class, so it's the same for services, etc.). It can be difficult to track what is used and where. Also by default, everything is mutable, so it can be difficult to track what is really edited during the component lifecycle.

To improve that, it's recommanded to make every method private by default, and every properties private readonly. If you need to access it from the template you can use protected. You should use public only for inputs.
In this example, we can make protected readonly the people property as it has only read access and have access from the template.

If we make it private, we will have an Angular compilation error "Property 'people' is private and only accessible within class 'PersonList'." so we will know instantly what we should do to fix it this.
Make small components
As much as possible, we should make small components.
Small components does not means every time reusable components, but small concern.
Here we can extract the person card into a separate component. It have a single responsibility, and if the card component grow up we will be able to manage it more easily.

Also prefer single file component (SFC). It will force you to keep component small (big component imply big file with SFC) and also let you see the full code of one component without switching files (in the example, we have only a small template, and the component stay easily readable).
Don't forget a component is only a class and it's not mandatory to have a dedicated file for each component. If it makes sense to keep component together, keep them together.
Use input.required<Type>() as much as possible
A lot of component cannot work without some or all of their inputs. Make them required explicitly as much as possible to make it verified compiled-time.
If you do so, you will gain two things:
Compiler will help you to set the minimal inputs list to a component;
Compiler will help you to find any wrong usage when you add the required;
Use the platform

A lot of thing can be done with plan HTML, or plain CSS or at last plain JavaScript. Using framework specific libraries will lock you with the maintainer and can be painful in the future. Platform API is stable from the last decades.
For example: cookies, storages, styles do not need libraries in my humble opinion.
In this example we use plain CSS to have different border color in light or dark theme.
Use the correct typing
Ensure to type every data sources to ensure TypeScript will help you to avoid a lot of errors.
In this example, we defined a Person interface which is used rather than any.
Use Angular naming conventions

In Angular, we may use app prefix selector follow by a kebab-case name for component or camelCase for directive. This is inherited from the Web Component specification and we should follow this rather it works without it.

Follow this convention allow any developer to know which tags in our template is a component from the app, a component from a lib (most of libs have a dedicated prefix, like Angular Material which use mat-then-kabab-case for component or matThenCamelCase for directive)
Also, prefer name your variables/properties in camelCase except for constants ones where you should prefer SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE.
Remember the casing names:
kebab-case is all lower case letters with words separated with dash like if a kebab spit was traversing the words (often used in HTML or CSS);
snake_case is all lower case letters with underscore between words like a crawling snake;
camelCase is first lower letter then a capital first letter for every next word (for JavaScript/TypeScript variables) like the hump from a camel;
PascalCase is a capital first letter for every words (historically it comes from the Pascal language);
There is also SCREAMING variants:
SCREAMING-KEBAB-CASE that I only seem in COBOL or SQL;
SCREAMING_SNAKE_CASE which is very common convention for constants;
Use computed for formatting
Naive formatting will be done in the template. When it become more complex, traditional Angular coding style will encourage you to use Pipes.
If you don't need to reuse the formatting logic, in modern Angular codebase, prefer computed() to have something simpler and when you need to reuse the formatting logic extract it to a Pipe.
You can use pipes in yours computed if you need it: computed(() => new DatePipe().transform(this.person().birthdate, 'dd/MM/yyyy')) will work if you need it.
